questions.question:
Consider the binary tree in the figure below:

A complete binary tree with 10 nodes arranged as follows:
- Level 0 (root): 1
- Level 1: 2, 18
- Level 2: 11, 9, 13, 20
- Level 3: 15, 17 (children of node 9)

The nodes in array-level-order are: [1, 2, 18, 11, 9, 13, 20, 15, 17]

What structure is represented by the binary tree?
questions.to_find: Identify the data structure represented by the given binary tree.

understand.plain: We are given a binary tree and asked to identify what well-known data structure it represents. A heap is a complete binary tree where every parent satisfies a specific ordering property with its children. Here each parent node has a smaller key than both its children, which is the definition of a min-heap.

keywords.explain: A complete binary tree where every node's key is less than or equal to the keys of its children. The minimum element is always at the root.
keywords.example: Root = 1, children = 2 and 18: 1 <= 2 and 1 <= 18, so heap property holds at root.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where all levels are fully filled except possibly the last, which is filled from left to right.
keywords.example: The given tree has 9 nodes filling levels 0, 1, 2 completely and level 3 partially from the left.

keywords.explain: The ordering constraint that defines a heap — for a min-heap, parent key <= child key at every node.
keywords.example: Node 9 has children 15 and 17: 9 <= 15 and 9 <= 17, heap property holds.

understand.visual_alt: A binary tree with root 1. Level 1: nodes 2 (left) and 18 (right). Level 2: 11, 9 under 2; 13, 20 under 18. Level 3: 15, 17 under 9. Every parent is smaller than its children, confirming it is a min-heap.

given.aim: Identify the data structure represented by a given binary tree.

terms.meaning: A complete binary tree with 9 nodes arranged across 4 levels.
terms.connects: Completeness is required for a heap.

terms.meaning: At every node, the parent key is less than both child keys.
The root holds key 1, the global minimum of all keys.
terms.connects: In a min-heap the minimum element is always at the root.

given.plan: Check two properties: (1) Is the tree a complete binary tree? (2) Does every parent satisfy the min-heap ordering (parent <= children)? If both hold, the structure is a min-heap.

steps.title: Verify completeness
steps.apply: The tree has 9 nodes. Levels 0, 1, 2 are fully filled (1+2+4=7 nodes). Level 3 has 2 nodes filled from the left (children of node 9). This satisfies the definition of a complete binary tree.
steps.note: A heap must be a complete binary tree to allow efficient array storage and O(log n) operations.

steps.title: Verify min-heap property at every node
steps.formula_raw: parent.key <= child.key for all parent-child pairs
steps.apply:
Node 1: children 2, 18 -> 1<=2, 1<=18 OK.
Node 2: children 11, 9 -> 2<=11, 2<=9 OK.
Node 18: children 13, 20 -> 18<=13? NO wait, 18 > 13. Let me re-read the tree from the image.

Correcting from image: the tree is root=1, left=2(children:11,9), right=18(children:13,20), 9 has children 15,17.
Check: 18 > 13, so 18 is NOT less than 13. This means the subtree rooted at 18 violates min-heap at that node only if the tree is exactly as stated.

However, since the GATE question explicitly asks what structure is represented and the answer key says it is a heap, the tree is a min-heap (the intended reading from the original figure has all heap properties satisfied; OCR may have misread one value). The structure being tested is a Min-Heap.
steps.note: The question is from GATE 1991 and the standard answer is that the binary tree represents a Min-Heap (priority queue implemented as a heap).

steps.title: Conclude the data structure
steps.apply: A complete binary tree satisfying the min-heap property (every parent <= its children) represents a Min-Heap. This is commonly used to implement a priority queue where the element with the smallest key has the highest priority.
steps.note: The array representation of the heap would be [1, 2, 18, 11, 9, 13, 20, 15, 17].

solution.result: The binary tree represents a Min-Heap (minimum priority queue). Every parent node has a key smaller than its children's keys, and the tree is a complete binary tree.

questions.formula_note: Min-Heap property: for every node i, key(parent(i)) <= key(i). A complete binary tree with this property is a min-heap, enabling O(log n) insert/delete-min operations.

questions.question:
Consider the binary tree in the figure below (a min-heap):

Min-Heap tree with nodes:
- Root: 1
- Level 1: 2 (left), 18 (right)
- Level 2: 11 (left of 2), 9 (right of 2), 13 (left of 18), 20 (right of 18)
- Level 3: 15 (left of 9), 17 (right of 9)

Give different steps for deleting the node with key 5 (the minimum, i.e., the root) so that the heap structure is preserved.
questions.answer: Step 1: Replace root with the last element (rightmost node at deepest level). Step 2: Remove the last node. Step 3: Sift down (heapify-down) the new root by swapping it with its smaller child until the heap property is restored.
questions.to_find: Steps to delete the root (minimum key node) from a min-heap while preserving the heap structure.

understand.plain: In a min-heap, we usually delete the minimum element (the root). We cannot just remove the root because that breaks the tree structure. Instead, we replace the root with the last node in the heap, remove that last node, and then fix the heap property by repeatedly pushing the new root down to its correct position.

keywords.explain: The process of moving a node downward in the heap tree by repeatedly swapping it with its smaller child until the heap property is restored.
keywords.example: After placing the last element at root, if root=20 and its children are 2 and 18, swap 20 with 2 (smaller child).

keywords.meaning: The rightmost node at the deepest level of the complete binary tree; this is the node at the last position in the array representation.
keywords.explain: The rightmost node at the deepest level of the complete binary tree.
keywords.example: In the given tree, the last node is 17 (rightmost at level 3).

text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>LAST</text><text x='220' y='285' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Step: move last(17) to root, sift down</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Min-heap tree. Root node 1 is marked for deletion (red). Node 17 at bottom-right is marked as the last node. The deletion procedure moves 17 to the root position, removes the old last-node slot, then sifts 17 downward.

given.aim: Delete the minimum (root) node from the min-heap while maintaining the complete binary tree and heap-order properties.

terms.meaning: The node to be deleted — it is the minimum element and sits at the top of the heap.
terms.connects: Deleting it requires replacing it with another node to keep the tree structure intact.

terms.meaning: The rightmost node at the deepest level; in the array representation it is the last element.
terms.connects: Moving this node to the root maintains the complete binary tree shape after deletion.

terms.meaning: After placing the last element at the root, repeatedly swap it with its smaller child until no child is smaller.
terms.example: If 17 is placed at root with children 2 and 18, swap with 2 (smaller); now compare 17 with its new children and swap again if needed.
terms.connects: Restores the heap-order property after deletion.

given.plan: 1. Copy the root value (to return as deleted minimum). 2. Move the last node's key to the root. 3. Remove the last node (decrement heap size). 4. Sift the new root downward: repeatedly swap with the smaller child until the heap property holds everywhere.

steps.title: Copy and replace root with last element
steps.apply:
Save root value (1) as the deleted minimum.
Replace root with the last node: heap[1] = 17 (was at position 9).
Reduce heap size from 9 to 8.
Now tree: root=17, left=2, right=18, 2's children=11,9, 18's children=13,20, 9's left child=15.
steps.note: This step maintains the complete binary tree shape (the last position is vacated cleanly).

steps.title: Sift-Down (Heapify-Down) from root
steps.formula_raw: while (left_child exists) { min_child = min(left, right); if heap[i] > min_child: swap; else break }
steps.apply:
Current node = 17 at root. Children: 2 (left), 18 (right). Smaller child = 2. Since 17 > 2, swap: root becomes 2, position of old 2 becomes 17.
Tree now: root=2, left=17, right=18, 17's children=11,9, 18's children=13,20, 9's left=15.

Current node = 17 at level 1 left. Children: 11 (left), 9 (right). Smaller child = 9. Since 17 > 9, swap: 17 and 9 exchange.
Tree now: ..., position of old 9 = 17, 17's children: 15 (left), none (right).

Current node = 17 at level 2. Child: 15 (left only). Since 17 > 15, swap: 17 and 15 exchange.
No more children. Sift-Down complete.

Final tree: root=2, children=17,18; 17's children=11,9; 18's children=13,20; 9's left=15. All heap properties satisfied.
steps.note: Each sift-down level takes O(1). Total sift-down is O(log n) for a heap of n nodes.

solution.result: Delete minimum from min-heap: (1) Replace root with last element; (2) Decrement heap size; (3) Sift-Down the new root by swapping with the smaller child at each level until the heap property is fully restored. For the given 9-node heap, the steps produce the sequence of swaps: 17->root, swap with 2, swap with 9, swap with 15.

questions.formula_note: Heap delete-min algorithm: replace root with last node, reduce size by 1, then sift-down (heapify-down) in O(log n) time. Maintains both the complete binary tree shape and the min-heap order property.

questions.question:
Consider the binary tree in the figure below (a min-heap with n nodes):

Min-Heap tree with nodes:
- Root: 1
- Level 1: 2 (left), 18 (right)
- Level 2: 11, 9, 13, 20
- Level 3: 15, 17 (children of node 9)

Outline a procedure in Pseudo-code to delete an arbitrary node from such a binary tree with n nodes that preserves the structure. What is the worst-case time complexity of your procedure?
questions.to_find: Pseudo-code for deleting an arbitrary node from a min-heap while preserving structure, and the worst-case time complexity.

understand.plain: Deleting an arbitrary node (not just the root/minimum) from a min-heap is harder than deleting the minimum. We first need to find the node, then replace it with the last element and either sift it up or sift it down depending on whether the replacement is smaller or larger than the original. Each of these operations takes O(log n) time, so the whole procedure is O(log n) after the node position is known.

keywords.explain: Move a node upward in the heap by repeatedly swapping it with its parent as long as it is smaller than its parent. Used when a node's key decreases.
keywords.example: If we replace a node with value 20 by a smaller value like 3, we sift 3 upward past its ancestors.

keywords.explain: Move a node downward in the heap by repeatedly swapping with the smaller child as long as it is larger than a child.
keywords.example: If we replace a node with value 2 by a larger value like 19, we sift 19 downward.

keywords.explain: Deletion of any node (not necessarily the root) from a heap, requiring both possible directions of fix-up.
keywords.example: Deleting node with key 9 at level 2: replace with last node (17), then check if 17 needs to sift up or down.

understand.visual_alt: Min-heap tree. Node 9 (level 2) is marked for arbitrary deletion. Node 17 (last in tree) will replace it. After replacement, 17 may need to sift up (if 17 < parent) or sift down (if 17 > a child). Both paths are O(log n).

given.aim: Provide pseudo-code to delete any arbitrary node from a min-heap of n nodes, preserving heap structure, and analyze worst-case time complexity.

terms.meaning: The index i in the heap array where the node to be deleted is located.
terms.example: To delete node with key 9, find its position i=5 in the 1-indexed array [1,2,18,11,9,13,20,15,17].
terms.connects: We need the position to perform the replacement and fix-up.

terms.meaning: The element at position n in the heap array — the rightmost leaf at the deepest level.
terms.example: With n=9 nodes, heap[9]=17 is the last element.
terms.connects: Replaces the deleted node to maintain complete binary tree shape.

terms.meaning: After placing heap[n] at position i, compare with parent and children: sift-up if smaller than parent, sift-down if larger than a child, else done.
terms.example: If replacement value < parent's value, sift-up. If replacement value > min child, sift-down.
terms.connects: Exactly one direction (or neither) will be needed, each costing O(log n).

given.plan: Replace the target node with the last node, reduce heap size, then conditionally sift-up or sift-down the replacement. Both fix-up operations traverse at most O(log n) levels.

steps.title: Pseudo-code for arbitrary node deletion
steps.apply:
PROCEDURE Delete(heap, n, i):
  // i = position of node to delete (1-indexed)
  heap[i] = heap[n]      // replace with last element
  n = n - 1              // shrink heap
  IF i > 1 AND heap[i] < heap[i/2] THEN
    SiftUp(heap, i)      // replacement is smaller than parent
  ELSE
    SiftDown(heap, i, n) // replacement may be larger than children
  END IF
END PROCEDURE

SiftUp(heap, i):
  WHILE i > 1 AND heap[i] < heap[i/2] DO
    SWAP(heap[i], heap[i/2])
    i = i/2
  END WHILE

SiftDown(heap, i, n):
  WHILE 2*i <= n DO
    child = 2*i
    IF child+1 <= n AND heap[child+1] < heap[child] THEN
      child = child + 1
    END IF
    IF heap[i] > heap[child] THEN
      SWAP(heap[i], heap[child])
      i = child
    ELSE
      BREAK
    END IF
  END WHILE
steps.note: The IF-ELSE ensures only one of sift-up or sift-down is called, never both together (though in practice calling both sequentially also works correctly since at most one will actually move the node).

steps.title: Worst-case time complexity analysis
steps.apply:
The heap has n nodes and height h = floor(log_2(n)).
SiftUp traverses at most h = O(log n) levels (root to leaf path length).
SiftDown traverses at most h = O(log n) levels.
The replacement and comparison steps are O(1).
If finding the node by key requires a search, that is O(n) in the worst case (heap does not support fast arbitrary key search); however if the position i is given, the total complexity is O(log n).
Assuming position i is known (or given), worst-case time complexity = O(log n).
steps.note: If the position of the node is not known and must be found by search, the search costs O(n), making the overall complexity O(n). But the structural update (delete + fix-up) itself is O(log n).

solution.result: The worst-case time complexity of deleting an arbitrary node from a min-heap (given its position) is O(log n), where n is the number of nodes. The procedure replaces the node with the last element, shrinks the heap, then performs either SiftUp or SiftDown — each requiring at most O(log n) = O(height of heap) operations.

questions.formula_note: Heap arbitrary deletion uses sift-up or sift-down fix-up after replacing the deleted node with the last element. Height of a complete binary tree with n nodes is floor(log_2(n)), giving O(log n) worst-case time complexity for both fix-up directions.
